request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"knewton::edtech","name":"knewton","one_liner":"Knewton built an adaptive learning platform for personalizing educational content, sold as a B2B SaaS API to publishers and universities in the US, and was acquired for under $17M in 2019 after raising over $157M.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"The new pitch is a B2C tiered freemium app targeting consumers directly; Knewton was a B2B SaaS platform licensing adaptive learning APIs to publishers and universities. The revenue logic, sales motion, and pricing structure are fundamentally different, though both involve recurring-use edtech products."},"market":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both operate in the personalized/adaptive learning space for students. Knewton worked on STEM and higher ed; the new pitch targets K-12 in Indonesia and the Philippines. The core proposition — AI-driven personalised instruction — overlaps meaningfully, but Knewton focused on enterprise US higher ed while the pitch targets consumer emerging-market K-12."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Knewton's GTM was entirely B2B: landing deals with Pearson, Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, and universities. The new pitch goes direct-to-consumer via an app with a freemium funnel and weekly live sessions — essentially the inverse GTM strategy."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Knewton raised over $157M across six rounds before a distressed acquisition; it was a heavily-funded US company. The new pitch appears to be at or near seed/early stage targeting emerging markets. Both face the same early challenge of proving learning efficacy at scale, but from very different positions."}},"why_similar":"Both are AI/adaptive-learning edtech products designed to personalise instruction for individual students. Knewton's core technology — tagging concepts at granular levels, performing real-time analysis of student performance data, and surfacing custom recommendations based on proficiency — is conceptually identical to the AI tutoring layer the new pitch describes. Both treat adaptive personalisation as the primary differentiator over generic content.","where_diverged":"At least four concrete dimensions separate them. (1) Customer type: Knewton was enterprise (B2B to publishers and universities); the new pitch is direct-to-consumer with individual families paying. (2) Geography: Knewton was US-centric (trusted facts confirm 'us'); the pitch targets Indonesia and the Philippines, emerging markets with lower willingness-to-pay but much faster smartphone-driven edtech adoption curves. (3) Business model: Knewton used subscription SaaS licensing to institutions; the pitch uses tiered freemium, meaning conversion from free to paid at consumer scale is the central monetisation challenge. (4) Product format: Knewton was a headless API/platform; the pitch wraps the AI layer in a consumer app augmented by weekly live small-group sessions, adding a human-delivery cost structure Knewton never had.","failure_causes":["B2B enterprise sales cycles too long and costly relative to revenue generated","Over-reliance on publisher partnerships that controlled distribution and margin","Pivot from consumer GMAT prep to API platform diluted brand clarity","Raised $157M+ but sold for under $17M — capital misallocation and burn rate","Inability to prove ROI to institutional buyers at scale","Commoditisation risk as publishers built or licensed competing adaptive tools","Failure to establish a direct relationship with end learners, leaving the company dependent on intermediaries"],"lessons_for_input":["Own the student relationship directly — never cede distribution to a single partner or platform that can cut you out, as Knewton learned when publisher relationships did not translate to defensible revenue.","Prove learning efficacy with measurable outcomes (pass rates, test scores) before scaling; Knewton's Arizona State results were compelling but never became a replicable proof-of-concept across its full product portfolio.","Size the freemium-to-paid conversion rate conservatively for Southeast Asian consumers — lower ARPU markets require volume and high retention to reach unit economics, so track CAC vs. LTV from day one.","The live small-group sessions are a genuine differentiator over a pure AI product, but they introduce a cost structure (tutor time, scheduling) that pure SaaS avoids — model this carefully before expanding cohorts.","Avoid over-raising before product-market fit; Knewton's later rounds locked it into a high-burn trajectory that made a sub-$17M distressed exit the only outcome despite world-class backing."]}
